Section 14.10
SPLITTING PATTERNS: PAIRS OF DOUBLETS
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

“Leaning” Doublets
Single vicinal protons next to one another give two distinct doublet signals (inequivalent geminal protons as well)
When the signals are close to one another, the “inner” peaks are abnormally tall

“Leaning” Doublets-1
Leaning becomes more extreme as peaks approach one another in chemical shift .

Section 14.11
COMPLEX SPLITTING PATTERNS

Complex Splitting Patterns
Protons vicinal or geminal to two or more inequivalent sets of 1 H are associated with complex splitting patterns
“________ et of ________ets”
For example, the signal for H a is split into a doublet of doublets due to the two inequivalent (diastereotopic) = CH 2 protons

Diastereotopic Protons
Upon replacement with a test group, diastereotopic protons give rise to diastereomers
Diastereotopic protons are inequivalent in NMR and are associated with distinct signals!
However, diastereotopic 1 H signals are often close in δ

Section 14.12
1 H NMR SPECTRA OF ALCOHOLS

Splitting by Hydroxyl –OH
In theory, the hydroxyl –OH proton should split adjacent protons into a doublet
For example, this effect is observed for benzyl alcohol

Exchange Effects: Missing Coupling
However, the –OH proton can exchange rapidly, especially at high [ROH], high T , and in the presence of acid or base
When exchange is rapid the –OH peak is not split by adjacent protons and does not split adjacent protons itself
The peak appears broad and can vary considerably in chemical shift (from δ 0.5 − 5 ) depending on [ROH]
Addition of D 2 O causes conversion to ROD, eliminating the hydroxyl proton peak

Section 14.13
NMR AND CONFORMATIONS

Conformation and 1 H NMR
Conformational changes often cause protons to change places…why don’t we observe distinct peaks for 1 H in different conformations ?

When conformational change is more rapid than the NMR timescale, we observe an averaging over conformations
Even though H x and H y are strictly diastereotopic, we observe only one peak because they interconvert rapidly (relative to the recording time of 1 H NMR)

Conformation and 1 H NMR-2
Conformational changes often cause protons to change places…why don’t we observe distinct peaks for 1 H in different conformations ?
At low temperatures, distinct peaks for axial and equatorial 1 H become visible !

Section 14.14
13 C NMR SPECTROSCOPY

13 C NMR Spectroscopy-1
Note the range of chemical shifts !

Section 14.15
13 C CHEMICAL SHIFTS

Factors Affecting 13 C Chemical Shifts
As for 1 H NMR signals, two factors affect the position of a 13 C NMR signal:
Electronegativity of nearby atoms
Hybridization of the carbon
Electronegative substituents cause deshielding and an increase in δ
This is an inductive effect that drops off with distance

Factors Affecting 13 C Chemical Shifts-1
As for 1 H NMR signals, two factors affect the position of a 13 C NMR signal:
Electronegativity of nearby atoms
Hybridization of the carbon
Carbons with sp 2 hybridization are less shielded than sp 3 carbons
However, sp carbons are more shielded than sp 2 carbons due to circulation of π electrons

Section 14.16
13 C NMR AND PEAK INTENSITIES

13 C Peak Areas are Misleading!
Integrations of 13 C NMR signals do not reflect the number of carbons associated with the signal
Each peak below corresponds to one carbon, but the heights and areas are vastly different
A consequence of the pulsed-FT method used to acquire 13 C spectra

Section 14.17
13 C – 1 H COUPLING
13 C NMR: Singlets Galore
